content:
Implementation of RecursiveMAS Playground, a browser-based interactive demonstration of the Recursive Multi-Agent Systems framework (Yang, Zou, et al., 2024). The implementation consists of two complementary systems: (1) recursiveMASWebLLM, a model compilation pipeline that exposes internal model states for latent-space communication, and (2) recursiveMASDemo, a JavaScript runtime that orchestrates local language models into collaborative recursion loops. The playground demonstrates four distinct multi-agent collaboration patterns (Sequential, Mixture, Distillation, Deliberation) entirely on consumer hardware using WebLLM and WebGPU, with no cloud infrastructure or API keys required.

1. Introduction

1.1 Problem Context

Standard multi-agent systems suffer from two critical inefficiencies:

вҖў Token Overhead: Intermediate agents must decode reasoning to natural language, which is passed wholesale to the next agent. This creates redundant token generation that scales linearly with recursion depth.

вҖў Training Inefficiency: Text-based agent interactions break the gradient flow during backpropagation, preventing end-to-end optimization of the multi-agent system as a unified computational graph.

The RecursiveMAS framework (Yang et al., 2024) addresses both by enabling agents to collaborate directly in latent spaceвҖ”the high-dimensional continuous representation space where models process meaning before converting to text.

1.2 Implementation Objectives

This implementation achieves three goals:

вҖў
Accessibility: Bring latent-space multi-agent research to consumer hardware via browser deployment.

вҖў
Transparency: Provide a visual, interactive tool that makes multi-agent recursion patterns understandable and inspectable.

вҖў
Fidelity: Reproduce the paper's key efficiency claims (accuracy gains, token savings, speed improvements) on real local models.

1.3 Key Innovation

Stock browser LLM frameworks (e.g., WebLLM) expose only the text I/O interface (input_ids вҶ’ logits). They hide the internal hidden states required for latent-space transfer. This implementation patches the MLC-LLM compiler to expose a get_last_hidden function, enabling true latent-vector transfer directly in the browser while maintaining backward compatibility with existing WebLLM workflows.

2. Architecture

2.1 System Components

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

2.2 Two-Repository Design

2.2.1 recursiveMASWebLLM: Model Build Pipeline

Purpose: Compile a WebGPU model graph with exposed latent-state functions.

Key Challenge: WebLLM models (via MLC-LLM вҶ’ TVM вҶ’ WebGPU) normally compile to a sealed graph: input_ids вҶ’ prefill вҶ’ logits. There is no intermediate access to last-layer hidden states.

Solution:

вҖў Patch the MLC-LLM model definition (e.g., Qwen2LMHeadModel) to add two new functions:

вҖў
get_last_hidden(input_embed, paged_kv_cache) вҶ’ last-layer hidden states [1, seq_len, hidden_size]

вҖў
decode_last_hidden(input_embed, paged_kv_cache) вҶ’ single-step variant [1, 1, hidden_size]

вҖў Re-register these in the MLC spec and recompile via mlc_llm compile --device webgpu.

вҖў Publish the .wasm module to a GitHub Release and quantized weights to Hugging Face.

GitHub Actions Workflow:

вҖў Installs MLC nightly SDK (CPU-only; compilation is code generation, not GPU execution)

вҖў Applies the patch (expose_hidden.py)

вҖў Runs convert_weight + gen_config + compile (all CPU)

вҖў Uploads .wasm to Release, weights to HF

вҖў Optionally trains RecursiveLink weights (offline PyTorch) on a provided dataset

Limitations:

вҖў Small models only (~0.5вҖ“1.5 GB, due to GitHub Actions disk limits)

вҖў Nightly MLC-LLM API is unstable; patch anchors require frequent validation

вҖў Training RecursiveLink is optional and GPU-dependent

2.2.2 recursiveMASDemo: Browser Orchestration Runtime

Purpose: Load a latent-exposing model and orchestrate the recursive agent loop.

Capabilities:

вҖў
Backbone picker: Select from WebLLM prebuilt models or custom latent-exposing builds

вҖў
Pattern selector: Choose Sequential, Mixture, Distillation, or Deliberation

вҖў
Recursion depth: Configure the number of rounds

вҖў
Comparison mode: Run the same query via both RecursiveMAS (latent) and text-MAS (baseline) side-by-side

вҖў
Visualization: Animated loop state, round counter, agent transcript, token/time metrics

3. Technical Foundations

3.1 RecursiveLink Mathematics

The RecursiveLink is a two-layer residual projection module, parameterized by:

$$\mathcal{R}(h) = W_3 h + W_2 \sigma(W_1 h)$$

Where:

вҖў $h$ = last-layer hidden state from a source agent (shape: [seq_len, hidden_dim] or [1, hidden_dim] for pooled)

вҖў $W_1$ = linear projection: $d_{\text{source}} \to d_{\text{bottleneck}}$ (e.g., 4096 вҶ’ 256)

вҖў $\sigma$ = GELU activation function

вҖў $W_2$ = linear projection: $d_{\text{bottleneck}} \to d_{\text{target}}$ (e.g., 256 вҶ’ 3584)

вҖў $W_3$ = residual branch: $d_{\text{source}} \to d_{\text{target}}$ (or identity if dims match)

Two variants:

вҖў Inner Link ($\mathcal{R}_{\text{in}}$): Used within a single agent. $W_3$ is typically Identity(), allowing the agent to feed its own latent output back as input for the next token step.

вҖў Outer Link ($\mathcal{R}_{\text{out}}$): Bridges heterogeneous models. $W_3$ performs dimension matching; $W_1, W_2$ perform semantic alignment.

Why Residual?

вҖў The residual path $(W_3 h)$ preserves the raw semantic content.

вҖў The non-linear path $(W_2 \sigma(W_1 h))$ fine-tunes for structural differences (tokenization, architecture-specific quirks).

вҖў Together, they stabilize training by ensuring core information flows through unchanged.

3.2 Latent Transfer in the Browser

Standard WebLLM pipeline:

text вҶ’ tokenize вҶ’ embedding lookup вҶ’ model forward (KV cache) вҶ’ logits вҶ’ sample

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

RecursiveMAS modification:

[Round t-1] Final Hidden State (vector)
вҶ“
[RecursiveLink.apply()]
вҶ“
Projected Latent (vector)
вҶ“
[Convert to f16 token]
вҶ“
[Concatenate with role prompt embeddings]
вҶ“
[Round t] Model forward (get_last_hidden or decode)
вҶ“
Last Hidden State вҶ’ [Optional: Pool to 1D vector for carry-over]

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Float16 Encoding: Latent vectors are converted to IEEE-754 half-precision to fit as a single embedding token, minimizing sequence length overhead.

Pooling Strategy: Multi-token hidden states [seq_len, hidden_dim] are mean-pooled to a single vector [hidden_dim] for carry-over to the next agent.

3.3 RecursiveLink Training (Offline, PyTorch)

The train_recursivelink.py script executes a two-stage training loop:

Stage 1: Inner Loop (Warm-up)

вҖў Objective: Align $\mathcal{R}_{\text{in}}(h)$ with the input-embedding distribution of the base model

вҖў Loss: Cosine similarity between projected hidden and original embeddings

вҖў Steps: ~200 iterations on small example texts

вҖў Effect: Initialize the inner link to near-identity behavior

Stage 2: Outer Loop (Full System)

вҖў Unroll the multi-agent loop over $T$ recursion rounds

вҖў Forward pass: Sample text from dataset вҶ’ tokenize вҶ’ run agents via latent loops вҶ’ final agent decodes logits

вҖў Loss: Standard cross-entropy on final output

вҖў Backprop: Gradient flows through all RecursiveLink parameters; base model frozen

вҖў Epochs: Multiple passes to converge

вҖў Output: Trained weights exported as recursivelink.json

Frozen Base Models: To reduce training cost, the base LLMs themselves are not fine-tuned. Only the $W_1, W_2, W_3$ matrices of each RecursiveLink are learned. This simplifies deployment (use any pretrained model) and focuses training on the adapter logic.

4. Implementation Details

4.1 recursiveMASWebLLM: Build Steps

вҖў
Install MLC Nightly

pip install --pre -U -f https://mlc.ai/wheels mlc-llm-nightly-cpu mlc-ai-nightly-cpu

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

вҖў
Patch Model Definition

python expose_hidden.py --arch qwen2

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

This modifies the installed MLC-LLM's model file to register get_last_hidden and decode_last_hidden.

вҖў
Build Artifacts

./build.sh
# Runs: convert_weight вҶ’ gen_config вҶ’ mlc_llm compile --device webgpu

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Outputs: .wasm file (WebGPU graph) + weight shards

вҖў
Optional: Train RecursiveLink

python train_recursivelink.py --model Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct --rounds 2

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Outputs: recursivelink.json (WвӮҒ, WвӮӮ, WвӮғ matrices)

вҖў
Publish

вҖў
.wasm вҶ’ GitHub Release artifact

вҖў Weights вҶ’ Hugging Face Model Hub

вҖў
recursivelink.json вҶ’ GitHub Release artifact

4.2 recursiveMASDemo: Runtime Architecture

4.2.1 Main Entry Point (main.js)

// Load backbone model
const model = await engine.initModel(modelId);
// For each recursion round
for (let round = 0; round < recursionDepth; round++) {
for (const agent of agents) {
// Latent path (if exposesLatent)
if (latentMode) {
const hidden = await latentForward(engine, agent.prompt);
const projected = recursiveLink.apply(hidden);
// Inject into next agent
agents[nextIdx].latentCarry = projected;
}
// Text path (baseline)
else {
const text = await textForward(engine, agent.prompt);
agents[nextIdx].textCarry = text;
}
}
}
// Final agent: full decode
const final = await chainDecode(engine, finalAgent.prompt, finalAgent.latentCarry);

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

4.2.2 Latent Forward (latent-chain.js)

export async function chainForward(rt, prompt, latentCarry) {
// 1. Get runtime (vm, pipeline, get_last_hidden function)
const latentRt = getLatentRuntime(engine, modelId);
// 2. Build combined input: [latentCarry embedding] вҠ• [prompt embeddings]
const carriedEmbedding = latentToken(rt, latentCarry, dtype);
const promptEmbedding = await pipeline.tokenizer.embed(prompt);
const combined = torch.cat([carriedEmbedding, promptEmbedding]);
// 3. Forward WITHOUT LM head (using get_last_hidden)
const [hidden, kv_cache] = await vm.getFunction('get_last_hidden')(
combined, kv_cache
);
// 4. Pool and extract
const nextCarry = poolHidden(hidden);
return { nextCarry, hidden };
}

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

4.2.3 Collaboration Patterns

Each pattern defines:

вҖў
Agent roles with heterogeneous model assignments (from paper Table 1)

вҖў
Agent prompts (e.g., Planner, Critic, Solver)

вҖў
Agent flow (sequential chain, parallel branches, etc.)

Sequential (рҹ”—): Planner вҶ’ Critic вҶ’ Solver

Planner decomposes; Critic judges; Solver refines. Each round refines the solution.

Mixture (рҹ§©): Math, Code, Science agents run in parallel; Summarizer aggregates.

Agents reason independently; final round's Summarizer sees all latent outputs.

Distillation (рҹҺ“): Expert вҶ’ Learner

Expert reasons fully; Learner (smaller model) takes expert's lat
